heroOverview: Enterobiasis is the most prevalent intestinal helminth infection in high-income countries, caused by Enterobius vermicularis — a 1-centimetre white roundworm that lives exclusively in the human colon and rectum. Globally, an estimated 500 million people are infected at any one time, with children aged 5-14 accounting for the majority of cases. The hallmark is intense perianal itching at night, when the female worm migrates to the anal margin to deposit eggs. Transmission is fecal-oral, primarily through hand-to-mouth transfer of microscopic eggs that cling to bedding, clothing, and fingernails. Most infections cause no serious harm, and a single dose of albendazole or mebendazole clears over 90% of cases, but household reinfection is common without simultaneous treatment of all contacts.

keyStats.prevalence: An estimated 500 million people infected worldwide at any one time; the most common helminth infection in temperate, high-income countries (WHO)
keyStats.demographics: Peak incidence in school-aged children 5-14 years; household and institutional clusters common in all income settings
keyStats.avgAge: Most common in children aged 5-14; all ages can be infected
keyStats.globalCases: Up to 1 billion people infected per year globally according to some estimates

definition: Enterobiasis (ICD-10: B80), also known as oxyuriasis or pinworm infection, is an intestinal parasitic infection caused by Enterobius vermicularis, a small nematode (roundworm) belonging to the family Oxyuridae. The adult worms inhabit the cecum, appendix, and proximal colon. Fertilised female worms — approximately 8-13 mm long — migrate nocturnally to the perianal skin, where they deposit between 10,000 and 20,000 eggs. The eggs become infective within 4-6 hours at body temperature and survive on environmental surfaces for up to 3 weeks. Infection occurs by ingestion of embryonated eggs via contaminated hands, food, bedding, or briefly airborne particles shed from infested clothing. After ingestion, eggs hatch in the small intestine, larvae migrate to the cecum, and mature adults appear within 2-4 weeks. The infection is classified under infectious diseases and managed by infectious disease specialists, paediatricians, or family physicians. Unlike most other helminths, Enterobius does not undergo tissue migration, so systemic complications are rare. The primary burden is the nocturnal perianal pruritus caused by egg-laying, which disrupts sleep and can propagate household transmission through scratching, nail contamination, and environmental egg dispersal.

typesClassification.description: Many infected individuals, particularly adults with low worm burdens, carry the parasite without any symptoms; they can nonetheless transmit infection to household contacts.
The classic presentation with intense perianal itching at night, disturbed sleep, irritability, and variable abdominal discomfort caused by higher worm burdens in the colon.

Rare migration of worms to atypical sites including the vagina, fallopian tubes, peritoneum, and, exceptionally, the liver; causes vulvovaginitis, pelvic inflammatory symptoms, or peritoneal granulomata.

primarySymptoms: Intense itching or crawling sensation around the anus at night (nocturnal perianal pruritus), caused by female worms depositing eggs at the anal margin; this is the defining symptom in over 80% of symptomatic cases.
Disturbed sleep, restlessness, and repeated waking, directly resulting from the nocturnal pruritus; prolonged infection leads to cumulative sleep deprivation and daytime fatigue.
Perianal skin excoriation and redness from chronic scratching, sometimes progressing to secondary bacterial infection of the broken skin.
Irritability, difficulty concentrating, and behavioural changes in children, attributed to poor sleep quality and, possibly, the host immune response to the parasite.
Mild diffuse abdominal pain or cramping, particularly around the navel or right lower quadrant, from intestinal irritation by adult worms in the cecum.
Nausea and intermittent loose stools or diarrhoea in some patients with higher worm burdens, though bowel habit change is less specific than perianal pruritus.
Visible white thread-like worms, approximately 1 cm long, on the perianal skin at night or in stools — their appearance is diagnostic when noticed by a parent or caregiver.
Vulvar or vaginal irritation, discharge, and itching in young girls, caused by worms migrating from the perianal area into the vaginal introitus.
Enuresis (bedwetting) in previously continent children has been reported in association with enterobiasis, though a direct causal mechanism is uncertain.
Weight loss and poor appetite in children with heavy, chronic, untreated infections, likely compounded by the associated sleep disturbance.
earlyWarningSigns: A child complaining of itching around the bottom specifically at night or after going to bed.
Restless sleep and repeated nighttime waking without another clear cause.
Unexplained irritability or poor concentration beginning in the school year, when transmission in classrooms is common.
A parent noticing small white threads in the child's underwear or on the perianal skin in the morning.
emergencySigns: Signs of secondary perianal bacterial infection: spreading redness, warmth, swelling, or purulent discharge from excoriated skin.
Severe pelvic or abdominal pain in a girl or woman, which may indicate ectopic migration to the fallopian tubes or peritoneum.
Symptoms of appendicitis (right lower quadrant pain, fever, rebound tenderness) — pinworms are an occasional incidental finding in the appendix and may contribute to inflammation.
High fever with pelvic signs in a female patient without another clear source, warranting assessment for ectopic enterobiasis.

causes.description: The dominant route: a person scratches the perianal area, eggs adhere under fingernails, and subsequent hand-to-mouth contact — or handling food without handwashing — delivers infective eggs to the digestive tract. Eggs hatch in the duodenum and larvae mature to adults in the cecum within 2-4 weeks.

causes.cause: Environmental egg dispersal from bedding and clothing
causes.description: Female worms deposit eggs at the anal margin; when the infected person moves or changes, eggs scatter onto sheets, pyjamas, towels, and underwear. Other household members contact these eggs and ingest them while dressing or handling laundry, sustaining household cycles of transmission.

Enterobius eggs are approximately 50-60 micrometres long and can become transiently airborne when bedding or clothing is shaken; inhalation followed by swallowing is a recognised but minor secondary route, particularly in crowded or poorly ventilated institutional settings.

causes.cause: Direct person-to-person contact in institutional settings
causes.description: In schools, daycare centres, and residential facilities, the high density of young children with limited hand hygiene and shared toilets, toys, and surfaces dramatically amplifies fecal-oral transmission, producing the cluster outbreaks that are characteristic of this infection.

In rare circumstances, larvae hatching from eggs deposited at the anal margin may migrate retrogradely back into the rectum without an external transmission step, potentially sustaining heavy worm burdens in highly susceptible individuals despite environmental hygiene measures.

riskFactors.factor: School or daycare attendance in young children
riskFactors.description: Children aged 5-14 attending school or daycare show the highest prevalence globally; shared toilets, toys, and hand-to-mouth behaviour create efficient transmission networks.

riskFactors.factor: Household contact with an infected person
riskFactors.description: Living with an infected child is the strongest predictor of adult infection; household attack rates can reach 50-75% when only one member is treated rather than all contacts simultaneously.
Crowding and shared bathroom facilities sustain transmission chains; outbreaks in care homes for people with developmental disabilities are well documented.
Children have higher hand-to-mouth behaviour, more frequent nail-biting, and less consistent handwashing than adults, substantially increasing egg ingestion per exposure.
Eggs persist under fingernails for hours to days; habits that bring nails to the mouth provide repeated self-inoculation opportunities and hinder decontamination.
Washing hands with soap and water removes eggs effectively; households or settings with limited handwashing facilities have substantially higher transmission rates.

People receiving corticosteroids, chemotherapy, or with primary immunodeficiency may harbour higher worm burdens and experience more severe symptoms, though serious systemic disease from enterobiasis is still rare.

affectedDemographics: School-aged children 5-14 years old, the primary reservoir and highest-prevalence group in all settings
Parents and caregivers of infected children, who are frequently reinfected from the household
Residents and staff of daycare centres, schools, and boarding institutions
People of all income levels; pinworm is as common in high-income countries as in low-income ones, unlike most other helminths
Individuals with nail-biting habits or difficulty maintaining hand hygiene
Residents of care facilities for people with intellectual disabilities
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of enterobiasis is primarily clinical and microbiological. The history of nocturnal perianal pruritus in a school-aged child is strongly suggestive, and a parent's report of seeing small white worms on the perianal skin at night is diagnostic on its own. When confirmation is needed, the cellophane tape test (also called the Scotch tape or sticky tape test) is the standard method: a piece of transparent adhesive tape is pressed against the perianal skin in the early morning before bathing or defaecation, then applied to a glass slide and examined under a low-power microscope; the characteristically asymmetrically flattened eggs, measuring approximately 50 × 20 micrometres, confirm the diagnosis. A single tape test detects about 50% of infections; three tests on consecutive mornings detect over 90%. Standard stool ova and parasite examination is unreliable because female worms deposit eggs on perianal skin rather than in the lumen, so eggs are rarely found in faeces without deliberate perianal sampling. Blood tests are not diagnostic; peripheral eosinophilia, when present, is typically mild or absent. The main clinical differential is other causes of perianal pruritus: anal fissure, haemorrhoids, contact dermatitis, psoriasis, vulvovaginal candidiasis, and, in children, sexual abuse — which requires careful clinical assessment when vaginal involvement is prominent. In suspected ectopic enterobiasis, abdominal ultrasound, laparoscopy, or histological examination of resected tissue may be required.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects Enterobius eggs on the perianal skin, the standard and most sensitive non-invasive diagnostic method.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: On waking, before bathing or defaecating, a piece of transparent adhesive tape is pressed firmly against the perianal skin, then applied to a glass slide. The doctor examines it under a microscope. Three morning samples on consecutive days detect over 90% of infections.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms the diagnosis when a parent or clinician observes worms on perianal skin at night or in a fresh stool specimen.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: No special test required; a torch examination of the perianal area 1-2 hours after the child falls asleep often reveals 1 cm white mobile worms. The finding is diagnostic.

diagnosticTests.test: Stool microscopy for ova and parasites
diagnosticTests.purpose: May occasionally detect Enterobius eggs or worms, though it has low sensitivity and is not the first-choice test for this parasite.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A stool sample is collected in a container and sent to the laboratory. A negative result does not exclude enterobiasis; tape testing is preferred.

diagnosticTests.test: Microscopic examination of perianal skin scraping
diagnosticTests.purpose: An alternative to the tape test in settings where adhesive tape is unavailable; a swab pressed against the perianal skin is examined microscopically.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The procedure is the same as the tape test; the swab is sent directly to the laboratory.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Not diagnostic of enterobiasis specifically but can support a general parasitic infection workup when the presentation is atypical.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; mild eosinophilia may be present but is neither sensitive nor specific for pinworm.

treatmentOverview: Enterobiasis is highly curable with a single oral dose of either albendazole (400 mg) or mebendazole (100 mg or 500 mg). Both agents achieve cure rates above 90% in individual patients. Because the worm lives exclusively in the gut lumen and does not invade tissue, anthelmintic therapy is straightforward and systemic side effects are minimal. The key challenge is preventing reinfection: a second dose is routinely given 2 weeks after the first to kill any larvae that have hatched from residual eggs since the first treatment. All symptomatic household contacts — and in many guidelines, all household members regardless of symptoms — are treated simultaneously; this approach substantially reduces household reinfection. Strict hygiene measures support but cannot replace anthelmintic therapy: washing bedding and clothing in hot water on the treatment day, daily showering, cutting fingernails short, and frequent handwashing eliminate environmental eggs. Pyrantel pamoate (11 mg/kg, maximum 1 g) is an alternative available over the counter in several countries. Mebendazole and pyrantel pamoate are generally preferred in pregnancy over albendazole, though anthelmintic therapy is usually deferred to the second trimester if clinically possible. Schools and daycare facilities do not need to be closed for a single case, but class-level screening and family notification are recommended when multiple cases cluster.

medicalTreatments.name: Albendazole
medicalTreatments.description: A single oral dose of 400 mg (200 mg for children under 2 years, used cautiously) kills adult worms in the gut lumen. A second dose is given 2 weeks later to cover newly hatched larvae. All household members are treated simultaneously. Albendazole is well tolerated; mild nausea or abdominal pain occurs in fewer than 5% of recipients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Single-dose cure rate approximately 90-95% for individual patients; exceeds 95% with the two-dose regimen.

medicalTreatments.name: Mebendazole
medicalTreatments.description: A single oral dose of 100 mg (or 500 mg in some guidelines) with a repeat at 2 weeks. Poorly absorbed systemically, acting locally within the gut lumen; this makes it very safe with minimal systemic side effects. Widely available and used as first-line in many countries.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Single-dose cure rate approximately 90-95%; repeat-dose regimen with household treatment achieves sustained cure in over 95% of cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Pyrantel pamoate
medicalTreatments.description: An alternative oral anthelmintic, 11 mg/kg (maximum 1 g) as a single dose repeated at 2 weeks. Available over the counter in the United States and Australia. Acts by causing spastic paralysis of the worm. Preferred by some clinicians in pregnancy over albendazole.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates of approximately 85-95%, broadly similar to mebendazole and albendazole.

medicalTreatments.name: Simultaneous household treatment
medicalTreatments.description: All household members are treated at the same time as the index case regardless of whether they report symptoms. This strategy is essential because up to 75% of household contacts harbour the parasite asymptomatically and will otherwise reinfect the treated patient.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces household reinfection rates by an estimated 50-70% compared with treating only the symptomatic individual.

alternativeTreatments.name: Strict hygiene measures as adjunct
alternativeTreatments.description: Washing all bedding, pyjamas, towels, and underwear in hot water (above 55°C) on the day of treatment kills eggs on fomites. Showering in the morning rather than bathing reduces egg ingestion from contaminated bathwater.

alternativeTreatments.name: Short nail maintenance
alternativeTreatments.description: Cutting fingernails very short and scrubbing under them daily reduces the amount of eggs that can accumulate and be transferred to the mouth; a useful adjunct to pharmaceutical treatment, especially in children.

alternativeTreatments.name: Topical relief for perianal pruritus
alternativeTreatments.description: Zinc oxide cream or a mild hydrocortisone 1% cream applied to the perianal area can temporarily relieve the intense itching while waiting for anthelmintic treatment to take effect, and prevents excoriation.

whySeeSpecialist: Most cases of enterobiasis are managed successfully in primary care or by parents using over-the-counter anthelmintics. An infectious disease specialist or paediatrician should be involved when infections recur repeatedly despite correct treatment and hygiene, when ectopic sites are suspected (pelvic pain, unexplained peritoneal findings, vaginal discharge in a girl refractory to topical treatment), when the patient is immunocompromised, or when an institutional outbreak requires co-ordinated public health management.
doctorSelectionGuide: For straightforward pinworm, a family physician or paediatrician is appropriate. If the infection fails to clear after two correctly administered treatment courses, or if ectopic enterobiasis is suspected, referral to an infectious disease specialist is warranted. For institutional outbreaks in schools or care settings, the local public health unit or infection prevention team should be engaged.
preventionStrategies: Wash hands thoroughly with soap and water after using the toilet and before meals; this is the single most effective preventive measure.
Keep fingernails short and discourage nail-biting and thumb-sucking in young children.
Shower in the morning (rather than bath) to wash away eggs deposited overnight.
Wash bedding, pyjamas, and underwear in hot water (above 55°C) weekly, and more frequently if active infection is occurring in the household.
Treat all household members simultaneously when one member is diagnosed, regardless of symptoms.
Change and wash underwear every morning to prevent egg dispersal through the day.
Reinforce handwashing and toileting hygiene in schools and daycare settings as routine practice.
lifestyleModifications: Establish a consistent morning hygiene routine: shower, change underwear, trim and scrub under fingernails.
Vacuum bedroom floors and wipe surfaces in the child's room on treatment day.
Avoid shaking bedding indoors, which disperses eggs; bundle and take directly to the washing machine.
Teach children the specific technique of handwashing: at least 20 seconds with soap, including under the nails.
Address nail-biting with behavioural strategies, as it is a strong independent risk factor for reinfection.
Do not share towels or toothbrushes during an active household outbreak.

recommended: A high-fibre diet to support regular bowel function, reducing any constipation that might increase perianal irritation
Adequate fluid intake to maintain normal bowel transit
Nutritionally balanced meals to support immune function during infection
avoid: No specific dietary restrictions are required for enterobiasis
Excessive sugar intake in children already prone to restless sleep, as it may compound fatigue from sleep disturbance

exerciseGuidelines: Physical activity does not need to be restricted with enterobiasis. Children should continue attending school and participating in sports. Perianal itching may make sitting still uncomfortable; gentle activity can distract from pruritus. Encourage swimming clubs and shared-equipment activities to adopt hygiene protocols (showering after pool use, not sharing towels) particularly during known household treatment periods.
dailyManagement: Give the full prescribed two-dose course of anthelmintic — the second dose at 2 weeks is not optional.
Treat all household members on the same days as the index case.
Wash all bedding and sleepwear on treatment day 1 and again on the day of the second dose.
Shower in the morning and change underwear daily until treatment is complete.
Track any new symptoms after completing both doses; persistent nocturnal itching beyond 4 weeks suggests reinfection rather than treatment failure.
Notify the school or daycare if multiple children are affected, so parallel treatment can be co-ordinated.
prognosis: The prognosis for enterobiasis is excellent. The infection causes no lasting systemic harm in the great majority of cases, and a correctly administered two-dose anthelmintic course combined with simultaneous household treatment achieves sustained cure in over 95% of patients. The principal challenge is reinfection from untreated contacts or persistent household environmental contamination rather than true treatment failure. Children who are repeatedly reinfected through the school years do not develop chronic organ damage or nutritional deficiency from enterobiasis alone. Ectopic enterobiasis is rare and usually resolves with anthelmintic treatment; surgical removal of granulomata is occasionally needed when worms have already died and encapsulated in the peritoneum or fallopian tubes. There is no chronic carrier state that persists without ongoing reinfection.
recoveryTimeline: Perianal itching typically diminishes within 3-7 days of the first anthelmintic dose as adult worms die. The second dose at 2 weeks targets larvae hatched since the first treatment and is essential for durable cure. Sleep quality usually improves within the first week as pruritus subsides. Full household decontamination — including repeat laundering and the second anthelmintic course — takes approximately 3 weeks from the first treatment day. Recurrence after that point indicates new reinfection from outside the household rather than treatment failure.
complications: Secondary bacterial infection of excoriated perianal skin from chronic scratching, occasionally requiring topical or oral antibiotics.
Ectopic migration to the vagina, fallopian tubes, or peritoneum causing vulvovaginitis, pelvic inflammatory disease, or peritoneal granulomata — rare but well documented in girls and women.
Sleep deprivation and its consequences — impaired school performance, behavioural problems, and daytime fatigue — in children with prolonged untreated infections.

Possible contribution to acute appendicitis: Enterobius worms are found in the appendix lumen in about 1-4% of appendectomy specimens, and while causality is debated, appendiceal obstruction by heavy worm loads has been reported.
Psychological distress and embarrassment in older children and adults, which can delay diagnosis and treatment-seeking.
Household disruption and parental anxiety from repeated reinfection cycles when all contacts are not treated simultaneously.

supportResources.description: Plain-English UK guidance on threadworm (pinworm) recognition, treatment, and hygiene from the National Health Service.

confusedWithConditions.name: Amebiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Amebiasis causes dysentery with bloody diarrhoea and abdominal cramping; perianal pruritus is absent, and diagnosis requires stool microscopy or antigen testing rather than a tape test.

confusedWithConditions.name: Trichuriasis (whipworm infection)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Whipworm causes rectal prolapse and dysentery in heavy infections; the hallmark is eggs in stool on microscopy, not perianal egg-laying, so the tape test is negative.

confusedWithConditions.name: Irritable bowel syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: IBS produces chronic abdominal pain and altered bowel habit without perianal pruritus, sleep disturbance, or visible worms; it is not caused by an organism and is not transmissible.

confusedWithConditions.name: Crohn's disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Crohn's disease causes chronic transmural gut inflammation with systemic features; it does not produce visible worms and is diagnosed on endoscopy and biopsy, not a tape test.

relatedConditions.name: Anisakiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another intestinal nematode infection, though acquired from marine fish rather than human contact; both present with gastrointestinal symptoms and are managed by infectious disease specialists.

relatedConditions.name: Trichostrongyliasis
relatedConditions.relevance: A related intestinal nematode infection with overlapping symptoms of abdominal pain and anaemia; differentiated by stool microscopy.

relatedConditions.name: Visceral larva migrans
relatedConditions.relevance: Another helminth infection in children, caused by Toxocara species; shares the demographic of young children but presents with eosinophilia and systemic features rather than perianal pruritus.

relatedConditions.name: Trichuriasis
relatedConditions.relevance: A co-endemic intestinal helminth in developing regions; both infect the large intestine and are treated with the same anthelmintic drugs.

faqs.question: What are the signs of pinworm infection?
faqs.answer: The main sign is intense itching around the anus that is worst at night, when female worms crawl out to lay eggs. Children also show disturbed sleep, irritability, and sometimes visible white thread-like worms on the skin around the bottom. Perianal redness from scratching is common.

faqs.question: How did my child get pinworms?
faqs.answer: Pinworms spread by swallowing microscopic eggs. Eggs cling to an infected person's hands, fingernails, bedding, and clothing, and reach a new host when hands touch the mouth. Children pick them up most often at school or daycare, where hand-to-mouth habits and shared surfaces make transmission easy.

faqs.question: Can adults get pinworms?
faqs.answer: Yes. Adults, particularly parents or carers of infected children, are frequently reinfected through household contact. Adults often carry pinworms without symptoms, which is why treating all household members — not only the child — is essential.

faqs.question: How is pinworm diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is usually made by the cellophane tape test: transparent tape is pressed against the perianal skin first thing in the morning before bathing, then placed on a glass slide and examined under a microscope for the characteristically shaped eggs. Seeing the worms on the skin at night is also diagnostic.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for pinworms?
faqs.answer: A single oral dose of albendazole (400 mg) or mebendazole (100 mg), repeated 2 weeks later, cures over 90% of cases. All household members should be treated at the same time. Bedding and clothes are washed in hot water on the treatment day to eliminate environmental eggs.

faqs.question: Why do I need to give a second dose of medicine?
faqs.answer: The first dose kills adult worms in the intestine but not eggs on the perianal skin or those already swallowed. Eggs hatch into new larvae within days. The second dose, given 2 weeks later, kills that next generation before they reach maturity and lay more eggs.

faqs.question: Does my whole family need treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes. Up to 75% of household contacts carry pinworms without symptoms. Treating only the person with symptoms and not the rest of the household almost always leads to rapid reinfection. All members living in the same home should take the anthelmintic on the same day.

faqs.question: How long does it take for pinworm symptoms to clear?
faqs.answer: Perianal itching usually lessens within 3-7 days of the first dose as adult worms die. Sleep quality improves within a week. Full cure requires the second dose at 2 weeks plus laundering of all bedding on both treatment days.

faqs.question: Can pinworms go away without treatment?
faqs.answer: A pinworm infection could theoretically clear if no new eggs were ingested after the current worms died (their lifespan is around 13 weeks), but in practice, ongoing reinfection through hand-to-mouth contact makes spontaneous clearance very unlikely. Treatment is simple, safe, and highly effective, so it is always recommended.

faqs.question: Are pinworms dangerous?
faqs.answer: For most people, pinworms cause discomfort and sleep disruption rather than serious illness. They do not cause lasting organ damage in healthy individuals. Rarely, worms migrate to the vagina or peritoneum in girls and women, causing more significant problems, but this is uncommon.

faqs.question: Should my child stay home from school with pinworms?
faqs.answer: Schools generally do not require exclusion for pinworm, because infection is so widespread and the incubation period is long. A child can return to or continue at school once treatment has started and good hand hygiene is in place. Notify the school so that others in the class can be checked.

faqs.question: Why does my child keep getting reinfected with pinworms?
faqs.answer: Reinfection usually means eggs are still present in the household — on unwashed bedding, clothing, or surfaces — or that another household member is untreated. Ensure all family members take both doses, launder bedding on both treatment days, and reinforce daily morning showers and handwashing after toileting.

faqs.question: Can pinworms cause appendicitis?
faqs.answer: Pinworms are occasionally found in the appendix at surgery and may in rare cases contribute to inflammation. However, the vast majority of appendicitis cases have other causes. If your child develops right lower abdominal pain with fever, seek medical care rather than attributing it to pinworms.

faqs.question: Can pinworms cause vaginal problems in girls?
faqs.answer: Yes. Female worms sometimes migrate from the anal area into the vagina, causing itching, discharge, and vulvovaginitis. This is more common in young girls. Anthelmintic treatment resolves most cases; persistent or recurrent vaginal symptoms in a girl with known pinworm should be evaluated by a paediatrician.

faqs.question: Is mebendazole safe in pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Mebendazole and pyrantel pamoate are generally preferred over albendazole for pinworm in pregnancy, but anthelmintic treatment is ideally deferred until after the first trimester if symptoms are tolerable. Discuss the timing and choice of treatment with your obstetrician or midwife.

faqs.question: What cleaning should I do at home after treating pinworms?
faqs.answer: On the day of each anthelmintic dose, wash all bedding, pyjamas, underwear, and towels in hot water above 55°C. Wipe toilet seats and door handles. Vacuum bedroom floors. Avoid shaking bedding indoors, as this disperses eggs into the air. These steps clear the environmental reservoir of eggs.

faqs.question: How do I do the tape test for pinworms?
faqs.answer: First thing in the morning, before the child bathes or uses the toilet, press a strip of transparent adhesive tape firmly against the skin around the anus. Remove it and stick it flat on a glass slide, then take it to your doctor or laboratory for microscopic examination. Repeat on three consecutive mornings for the highest detection rate.

faqs.question: Can pinworms affect sleep long term?
faqs.answer: During an active infection, nocturnal itching reliably disturbs sleep and can cause cumulative fatigue, irritability, and school performance problems in children. Once the infection is treated and the itch resolves — usually within one week — sleep returns to normal without lasting effects.

faqs.question: Are over-the-counter treatments for pinworms effective?
faqs.answer: Pyrantel pamoate, available over the counter in the US and Australia, is effective for pinworm with cure rates around 85-95% per treatment course. Over-the-counter mebendazole is available in some countries. Always give the second dose at 2 weeks and treat the whole household for best results.

faqs.question: What is the difference between pinworms and threadworms?
faqs.answer: They are the same organism. Pinworm is the name used in North America; threadworm is the common term in the UK, Australia, and other English-speaking countries. Both terms refer to Enterobius vermicularis, and the treatment is identical.

searchTags: how to get rid of pinworms
metaDescription: Pinworm infection causes intense perianal itching at night in children. Learn the tape test diagnosis, albendazole treatment, and how to prevent reinfection.
